Re
F ern P olrrrcel P necrrcps C oMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 19,2010
Jeffrey A. Walter
Walter & Pistole
670 West Napa Street, Suite F
Sonoma, CA95476
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-275
Dear Mr. Walter:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this
letter apply only to prospective actions. tn addition, this letter is based on the facts presented.
The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a findei of fact
when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because you have not
identified the person on whose behalf advice is being sought and have not limited your request
for assistance to a specific governmental decision, we are providing informal assisiance.2 
^
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law
conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QTJESTTON
May a city councilmember take part in city council decisions regarding the city's Farmers
Market despite receiving a $500 payment in 2008 and a $250 payment (which was returned) in
2009 from the operators of the market to book musicians to play during market events?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. AII statutory
retbrences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the f'air political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. AII
regulatory ret-erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
: Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fbrmal
written advice. (Section lt3l l4: Regulation 18329(cX3).)


File No. l-09-275
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Barring additional facts, the city councilmember may take part in city council decisions
regarding the city's Farmers Market, including a decision to selecith" op..uio* for the 2010
Farmers Market' if he completely and unequivocally renounces any intent to receive payments
for his musician booking services during the 2010 Fu.rn"r, Market.
FACTS
You are the interim city attomey for the City of Sonoma (the "City") and are requesting
advice on the behalf of a member of the Sonoma City Council (the "City'Council',). rfre city"
permits an organization to operate a Farmers tvlarkeiin a portion of the "City ,quur. from Apiil to
october of each year. The City has issued requests for proposal for the zoio rarmers Market
season and is soliciting-proposals to operate the Farmeri Vtart<et from those persons and
organizations interested and qualified to operate the market. Among other general decisions
pertinent to the Farmers Market, it is anticipated that the proposats witt be irbmitted to the City
Council for its decision and selection at thoCity Councilh meeting on January 20, ZOIO.
In respect todecisions regarding the Farmers Market, one councilmember has previously
performed services for previous operators of the market. More specifically, the councilmember
entered into an agreement in April 2008 with the operators of the Farmers-Market to book
musicians to play during market events. While the musicians played for free, the councilmember
was paid a total of $500 for his services during the 2008 Farmers Market season. The
councilmember was paid in two installments of $250 including one installment in April200g and
a second installment in September 200g.
In addition, the councilmember provided musician booking services during the 2009
Farmers Market season. However during this season, there was no oral or written agreement
between the councilmember and the operators of the Farmers Market conceming the
councilmember's services. While the councilmember intended to provide these services for free,
the operators delivered a check to the councilmember for $250 0n bctober 27,200g. This
payment was the only payment received by the Councilmember for services provided during the
2009 season, and the councilmember was told by representatives of the op"rutorc that they had
intended to pay the councilmember earlier in the year but failed to make tfr. puy-"nt after a
bookkeeping oversight. Nonetheless, the councilmember returned the organtaiion's $250
payment for the 2009 season on November 17,2OOg.
.{'I\ALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "fi-nancial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have


File No. l-09-275
Page No. 3
a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for
deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of inteiest in a given govemmental
decision.
Step One: Is the individual a,,public official?',
The Act's c9{tl9t-of-interest provisions apply to all "public officials." (Sections g7100,
87103; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) A "public officiai"is "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local govemment agency . . .." (Section 8204g.) As a member of the
City Council, the individual is a public official within the meaning of tfr" e"i.,
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official-'makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agieement on behalf of his or her
agency' (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participate. i., u gou"rnmental decision,, when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without signif[ant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence-a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or app_ears before any member, officer, employee, Lr consultant of his or her
agency' (Regulation 18702-.3(a).) A public official is also attempting to use his or her position
to influence a governmental decision when the official, for the purpose of influencing a decision
by another agency, acts or purports to act on his or her agen"y'i b"hulf before an official of the
other agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) As a member ol*r" City Council, the councilmember is
making, participating in making, or influencing a govemmental decision when taking part in a
city Council decisions regarding the Farmers Market including the decision to select the
operators for the 2010 Farmers Market.
Step Three: what are the officiaPs economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest,, in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
I If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*87z0ofilers" include city council members) and
he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public *""ting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each tyie or..onori. interest involved in the decision as well
as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regutaiion r szoz.sox r )(B), on rhe record of the meeting; (2)
recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room fbr the duration of the discussion and./or vote on the item. For
closed sessions, consent calendars' absences and speaking or u ,.ru., ofttre puulic regarainl personal interests,
special rules found in Regulation lg7o2.5,subdivisions (i) ana ral apply. (Section g7105.)


File No. I-09-275
Page No.4
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the oflicial's1.orro.i. interests, described as follows:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section tizto:(u); Regularion 1g703.1(a)); or in which he
or she is a director,.officer, p{tner, trustee, employee, or hords any position of
managemenr. (Secrion 97103(d); Regulation lg70J. l@).)
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest
of $2,000 or more. (Section sztb:G); Regulation rg703.2.)
o An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to tie decision. (Section g7103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)
o An economic interest in a source- of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 871037e); nJfiuiutio, rg703.4.)
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family. This is known is the "personal financial eifects,, rule. (Section
87 103 ; Regulation I 8703.5.)
As you have stated, the councilmember received a payment of $500 dollars for the
musician booking services he provided the operators of the Farmers Market in 200g and $250
(which was subsequently returned) in20o9. These facts implicate the following economic
interests:
Musician Booking Services
The Act broadly defines a "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated
for profit, including but not Iimited to aproprietorship, purtn".it ip, firm, business trust, joint
venture, syndicate, corporation or association." (Section szoos.y ^As 
the proria., of musician
booking services, the councilmember is an employee or manager of a business entity and has an
economic interest in.this business entity under Section 87103(d). In addition, if the
councilmember receives income of $500 or more for his services in the 12 months prior to the
govemmental decision, the councilmember will also have an economic interest in his business as
a source of income. (Section 87l}2(c).)
As the provider of musician booking services, the councilmember may also have an
economic interest in each of the clients puyi.rg for his services. Under Section g2030(a), if a
public'official owns a l0-percent interest or greater in a business, customers who are sources of
income to that business are also considered ,ou."", of income to the public official. provided


File No. l-09-275
Page No. 5
that the councilmember's pro rata share of the income derived from any particular client
aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision, the
councilmember has an economic interest in the client o, u ,ou.". of income. (Section g7103(c).)
From your account of the facts, the councilmember has not received income of $500 or
more in the past 12 months from the operators of the 2008 or 2009 Farmers Market.
Accordingly, you have not indicated an economic interest in any client of the councilmember,s
business as a source of income that may be affected by a future decision pertaining to the
Farmers Market.a
Personal Finances
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in
the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. (Sebtion g7103; Regulation 1g703.5.)
Step Four: Are the councilmember's economic interests direcfly or indirectly
involved in the decision?
Musician Bookine Services
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that-a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when ihat business entity or source of income,
either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2)Jt a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's agency.' A plrson is tlie subiect of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, deniai or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental
decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations lg704.l(b), 1g705.1(a)(2), and
a You have not identified any clients of the councilmember other than the operators of the 200g and 2oo9
Farmers Market' Therefore, we cannot analyzeany potential conflicts of interest the councilmember may have in a
decision affecting other clients. Prior to 
'noi.ing, piriicipating in making, or influencing any governmental decision,
the councilmember will need to determine wtreitrer ony ott.."rlier,r luuriry as a source of income and whether a
conflict of interest may arise based upon an economic interest in one orthese clients. If the councilmember has
additional questions related to an economic interest in a client other than the operators of the 200g and 200g Farmers
Market' he should request further assistance providing att reteranrracis.


File No. l-09-275
Page No. 6
Regulation 18705.3(b).) From the facts provided, the councilmember's business did not initiate
the proceedings and is not a party in, or iubject of, the proceedings within the meaning of
Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, the councilmemberis economic interest in his business as a
business entity, or as a source of income, is only tndtrectly involved in govemment decisions
regarding the Farmers Market.
Personal Finances
As for an official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, an official,s
economic interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly invoived in the
govemmental decision if facts iuggest any financial effect on the ""oro-i" interest. (Regulation
18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: Witl there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the individuals, economic interests? 
-
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, iiis necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Musician Booking Services
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision, including
business entities that are a source of income to an official, ihe materiality standard is given at
Regulation 18705. l(c).
The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the business.
While we do not know the size of the councilmem6er's business, Regulation lg7g5.l(cXa)
provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a buslness, not publicly traded
and relatively modest in economic sizi, is material if it is reasonably foreseeabL that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing e*plnses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, -orJ; or,
"(C) The governmentaldecision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity;s assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,




File No. l-09-275
Page No. 8
provided, the councilmember has been paid $500 or less annually for his past services provided
to the operators of the Farmers Market. Considering the past amount paid to the councilmember,
itdoes not appear reasonably foreseeable that goverimenial decisions regarding the Farmers
Market, including the decision to select the organization to operate the market, would materially
affect the councilmember's economic interestln his business.
Personal Finances:
Assuming that the councilmember does not have a direct or indirect investment in this
business of $2,000 or more, the personal financial effects rule prohibits the "our.it*"-u";;or,
making, participating in making, or influencing any decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of $250 or more on the councilmember,, p".ronui finances such as
income received or lost for musician booking services. under the facis you hur" provided, the
councilmember has been paid $500 for the sirvices he provided to the oierators of the Farmers
Market in 2008 and $250 (which was retumed) for the services he provided in 2009.
Considering-the previous payments to the councilmember alone, it appears to be
reasonably foreseeable that a govemmental decision to select the operato6 oi trr" 2010 Farmers
Market would have a material financial effect upon the councilmember's economic interest in his
personal finances if the operators of the 2008 oi zoog Farmers Market *" poi"*iul candidates to
operate the 2010 Farmers Market. In addition, it also appears to be reasonuuty foreseeable that a
govemmental decision to select the operators of the zoid Farmers Market *outo have a material
financial effect upon the councilmemter's economic interest in his personal finances if the
councilmember has expectations that any of the potential candidates to operate the 2010 Farmers
Market will seek musician booking.e*i"es similar to those he has preriiusfprovided.
However' you have also indicated that the councilmember returned the payment he
received for his services in2009, and there is no indication that the councilmember will be
seeking payments, or has been offered payments, for musician booking ,"*i* 
provided during
the 2010 Farmers tvtalke^t. Previously, *e haue determined that an otherwise material financial
effect is not reasonably foreseeable if the public official "completely and unequivocally
renounces any intent to avail him/trerself of the material finaniial benefit giuin! rise to a conflict
of interest." (Thompson Adviceletter, No. 4-02-024; LibowAdvice Letter, No. I-91-461; and
Newsom Advice Letter, No. 4-01-183.) Accordingly, if the councilmember completely and
unequivocally renounces any intent to receive puyir"ntr for his services during the 2010 Farmers
Market, there is no foreseeable material financial effect on his personal financls.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
^. 
Because you have not presented any facts indicating a reasonable foreseeable material
financial effect on any of the economic interests implicated] it is unnecerrury fo, us to consider
whether the governmental decision comes within un "^".ption to the conflict-of-interest rules at
this time.


File No. l-09-275
Page No. 9
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (gt6) 322_5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl

regarding the Farmers Market.

As for an official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, an official,s
economic interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly invoived in the
govemmental decision if facts iuggest any financial effect on the ""oro-i" interest. (Regulation
18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: Witl there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the individuals, economic interests? 
-
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, iiis necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Musician Booking Services
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision, including
business entities that are a source of income to an official, ihe materiality standard is given at
Regulation 18705. l(c).
The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the business.
While we do not know the size of the councilmem6er's business, Regulation lg7g5.l(cXa)
provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a buslness, not publicly traded
and relatively modest in economic sizi, is material if it is reasonably foreseeabL that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing e*plnses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, -orJ; or,
"(C) The governmentaldecision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity;s assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,

provided, the councilmember has been paid $500 or less annually for his past services provided
to the operators of the Farmers Market. Considering the past amount paid to the councilmember,
itdoes not appear reasonably foreseeable that goverimenial decisions regarding the Farmers
Market, including the decision to select the organization to operate the market, would materially
affect the councilmember's economic interestln his business.
Personal Finances:
Assuming that the councilmember does not have a direct or indirect investment in this
business of $2,000 or more, the personal financial effects rule prohibits the "our.it*"-u";;or,
making, participating in making, or influencing any decision that will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of $250 or more on the councilmember,, p".ronui finances such as
income received or lost for musician booking services. under the facis you hur" provided, the
councilmember has been paid $500 for the sirvices he provided to the oierators of the Farmers
Market in 2008 and $250 (which was retumed) for the services he provided in 2009.
Considering-the previous payments to the councilmember alone, it appears to be
reasonably foreseeable that a govemmental decision to select the operato6 oi trr" 2010 Farmers
Market would have a material financial effect upon the councilmember's economic interest in his
personal finances if the operators of the 2008 oi zoog Farmers Market *" poi"*iul candidates to
operate the 2010 Farmers Market. In addition, it also appears to be reasonuuty foreseeable that a
govemmental decision to select the operators of the zoid Farmers Market *outo have a material
financial effect upon the councilmemter's economic interest in his personal finances if the
councilmember has expectations that any of the potential candidates to operate the 2010 Farmers
Market will seek musician booking.e*i"es similar to those he has preriiusfprovided.
However' you have also indicated that the councilmember returned the payment he
received for his services in2009, and there is no indication that the councilmember will be
seeking payments, or has been offered payments, for musician booking ,"*i* 
provided during
the 2010 Farmers tvtalke^t. Previously, *e haue determined that an otherwise material financial
effect is not reasonably foreseeable if the public official "completely and unequivocally
renounces any intent to avail him/trerself of the material finaniial benefit giuin! rise to a conflict
of interest." (Thompson Adviceletter, No. 4-02-024; LibowAdvice Letter, No. I-91-461; and
Newsom Advice Letter, No. 4-01-183.) Accordingly, if the councilmember completely and
unequivocally renounces any intent to receive puyir"ntr for his services during the 2010 Farmers
Market, there is no foreseeable material financial effect on his personal financls.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
^. 
Because you have not presented any facts indicating a reasonable foreseeable material
financial effect on any of the economic interests implicated] it is unnecerrury fo, us to consider
whether the governmental decision comes within un "^".ption to the conflict-of-interest rules at
this time.

2009 from the operators of the market to book musicians to play during market events?
